title: The Install Request
description: Start here: this is the conceptual entry point of the whole system. These schemas have no executable main() — the story begins with the user-authored install configuration, the contract describing what someone wants installed (target harness, profile, modules, include/exclude selectors). It captures requested intent before any resolution happens, and every downstream schema exists to refine, satisfy, or record this request. Reading it first frames everything that follows as 'what happens to an install request.'
languageLesson: JSON Schema draft-07 files declare a stable identity with $schema (the dialect) and $id (this document's canonical URI). $id is what lets other schemas point at this contract with $ref, so treat it as the schema's permanent address — like a package name, it should not casually change.

title: Profiles: Coarse Presets
description: Building on the install request from Step 1, the config can name a profile rather than hand-pick everything. This schema defines those named presets (e.g. minimal, full) — each bundles a curated set of module ids. It is the coarsest layer of the selection pipeline: one word ('full') expands into many modules. This is where opinionated defaults live, so newcomers can install sensibly without understanding every module.

title: Modules: The Catalog Core
description: This is the most depended-upon schema in the entire graph — the install request, profiles, components, the install-state record, and the plugin manifest all reference it. It is the mid-tier catalog of installable modules (rules, agents, commands, hooks, platform, skills, docs) with their source paths, targets, dependencies, cost, and stability. Profiles bundle these and components map onto them, making modules the common currency the whole installer speaks. Understand this schema and the selection pipeline snaps into focus.
languageLesson: Catalog schemas like this lean on $defs (draft-07's reusable definition block) plus internal $ref to avoid repeating shared shapes — e.g. a single 'moduleId' or 'sourcePath' definition referenced from many places. Defining a shape once and $ref-ing it keeps the catalog consistent and is the JSON-Schema equivalent of extracting a shared type.

title: Components: User-Facing Units
description: Completing the selection pipeline, this schema is the most granular layer — the user-facing installable units (baseline, language, framework, capability, agent, skill, locale) that each map down to the underlying modules from Step 3. Where profiles are coarse bundles, components are the fine-grained vocabulary a person actually browses and picks from. Together, Steps 1-4 form the full request/catalog arc the project calls the Install Selection layer: config selects profiles, profiles bundle modules, components map to modules.

title: Install State: The Outcome
description: Once a request from Step 1 is resolved against the catalog and applied, the result is written here. This durable manifest (versioned ecc.install.v1) records the resolved target, the original request, how modules were resolved, source provenance, and the concrete file operations performed. It is the bridge between the selection pipeline and reality: it references both the original install-config and the modules catalog, closing the loop on 'what was actually done.' This and the package-manager preference form the project's Install Outcome & Persistence layer.
languageLesson: Note the embedded version tag 'ecc.install.v1' inside the payload, distinct from the file's $id. Versioning the data shape (not just the schema document) lets a reader detect which generation of record it is holding — essential for any persisted manifest that must survive schema evolution. Bump the version only on breaking shape changes.

title: Package-Manager Preference
description: A small but real part of the outcome layer: this schema records which package manager (npm/pnpm/yarn/bun) the user prefers and when that choice was set. It relates back to the install-config because the install/build flow consults it when fetching dependencies. It is a good example of how ECC isolates a single cross-cutting preference into its own tiny, focused contract rather than burying it inside a larger schema.
languageLesson: An enum constrains a field to a fixed closed set — here the four package-manager names. Enums turn 'any string' into a validated, self-documenting choice list, so an invalid value is rejected at the contract boundary instead of failing deep inside the build.

title: Runtime State Store
description: Here the tour crosses from install-time into run-time. This is ECC's central control-plane database (versioned ecc.state-store.v1): sessions, skill runs and versions, decisions, governance events, work items — and notably the embedded install state from Step 5, so the runtime always knows how it was set up. It is the backbone behind continuous-learning and governance. Where the previous schemas described how the system gets built, this one describes how the running system remembers and governs itself.
languageLesson: A large aggregate schema like this composes many sub-records under one root. Draft-07 uses 'properties' with 'required' to mark which keys must be present, and 'additionalProperties' to decide whether unexpected keys are allowed. Setting additionalProperties:false on a control-plane store is a deliberate strictness choice — it rejects malformed state rather than silently absorbing it.

title: Skill Provenance
description: Provenance attaches to the runtime control plane: it records where a learned or imported skill came from — origin source, creation time, confidence (0-1), and author. It is required alongside skills under ~/.claude/skills/learned/* and imported/*, and it links to the state-store's skillVersion records from Step 7. This is the trust-and-traceability piece of continuous learning: it lets the system say not just 'I have this skill' but 'here is where it came from and how confident I am in it.'
languageLesson: The confidence field is a bounded number, expressed with minimum:0 and maximum:1. Numeric range constraints push validation into the contract so an out-of-band score (say 1.7) is rejected at the edge — cheaper and safer than discovering it during a ranking computation later.

title: Plugin Manifest
description: Now we reach the artifacts the installer lays down into the target harness — the Harness Extension Config layer. This is the Claude plugin manifest: name, version, author, declared skills/commands/mcpServers, and a feature-count summary. It references the modules catalog from Step 3 because the installer reads it and writes it out for the target harness. It is the public face of an installed bundle inside the harness, the metadata that tells the host what capabilities just arrived.

title: Hooks Configuration
description: The capstone artifact: the Claude Code hooks configuration the installer lays down so the harness can extend its own behavior at runtime. It is event-keyed (matcher entries per event) and supports three distinct hook action types — command, http, and prompt/agent. Pair it with the plugin manifest from Step 9 and you have the complete picture: the selection pipeline (Steps 1-4) chooses what to install, the outcome layer (Steps 5-6) records and supports it, the control plane (Steps 7-8) governs it at runtime, and these final artifacts wire it into the host harness.
languageLesson: The three hook action types are a textbook oneOf — a discriminated union where a value must validate against exactly one branch (command vs http vs prompt/agent). oneOf (as opposed to anyOf) enforces mutual exclusivity, so a single hook entry cannot accidentally be two action types at once; the differing required keys per branch act as the discriminator.
